elements.sent-2411-001: Large language models are revolutionizing every aspect of human life.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, the unprecedented power comes at the cost of significant computing intensity, suggesting long latency and large energy footprint.
elements.sent-2411-003: Key-Value Cache and Semantic Cache have been proposed as a solution to the above problem, but both suffer from limited scalability due to significant memory cost for each token or instruction embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-004: Motivated by the observations that most instructions are short, repetitive and predictable by LLMs, we propose to predict user-instructions by an instruction-aligned LLM and store them in a predictive cache, so-called InstCache.
elements.sent-2411-005: We introduce an instruction pre-population algorithm based on the negative log likelihood of instructions, determining the cache size with regard to the hit rate.
elements.sent-2411-006: The proposed InstCache is efficiently implemented as a hash table with minimal lookup latency for deployment.
elements.sent-2411-007: Experimental results show that InstCache can achieve up to 51.34\% hit rate on LMSys dataset, which corresponds to a 2x speedup, at a memory cost of only 4.5GB.
elements.sent-2411-008: Recently Large Language Models (LLMs) as well as their multi-modal equivalents have become the essential driver of a new wave of technology innovation, revolutionizing every aspect of human life.
elements.sent-2411-009: The unprecedented power of LLMs, however, comes at the price of unparallel scale of computation, which incurs a series of challenges.
elements.sent-2411-010: One primary challenge is the energy footprint, which is dominantly consumed by GPUs and other AI acceleration hardware.
elements.sent-2411-011: It is estimated that by 2026 the total energy consumption of AI computation will amount to 5\% of US electricity production\cite{energy and the number will reach 20\% in 2028.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Another challenge is the latency of LLM response, which hinders a much wider application of LLM in live situations.
elements.sent-2411-013: A large body of research has been dedicated to addressing the LLM computation bottleneck by data reusing.
elements.sent-2411-014: Among these works, Key-Value (KV) Cache\cite{vllm, sglang lowers the amount of GPU computation without the loss of LLM inference performance by trading computation for memory according to the auto-regressive nature of the decoder.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Semantic Cache\cite{gptcache offers reuse opportunities by searching and retrieving similar queries\footnotemark[3] stored in a database.}
elements.sent-2411-016: While enabling significant savings in power consumption and latency, both methods are challenged by the scalability.
elements.sent-2411-017: For instance, key-value states of each token takes approximately a few megabytes of storage\cite{attentionstore , which limits KV Cache to reusing only the common instruction prefix or previous rounds of a dialogue\cite{sglang .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: On the other hand, Semantic Cache can reuse entire queries.
elements.sent-2411-019: However, scaling Semantic Cache remains challenging, as it requires searching for similar instructions by evaluating similarity between the embedding of incoming queries and stored instructions.
elements.sent-2411-020: Generating embedding and searching similar instructions incurs significant lookup latency and storage cost.
elements.sent-2411-021: Moreover, simple data reuse does not fully unlock the potential of data, particularly in the era of LLM.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{pic/Patterns.pdf} \caption{Analysis of instructions shows that most queries are short, repetitive and predictable with LLMs. The analysis of frequency and probability distribution is conducted on ShareGPT, with probabilities of tokens evaluated using Llama3-8B.} \label{fig:patterns}
elements.sent-2411-022: This work is inspired by the observation that a significant portion of instructions to LLMs consists of short instructions, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:patterns (a).}
elements.sent-2411-023: Furthermore, as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:patterns (b) and (c), these queries are often repetitive and predictable.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Based on these observations, we propose an efficient LLM caching system, InstCache, for LLM inference.
elements.sent-2411-025: The basic idea is to predict user-instructions by an instruction-aligned LLM and store them as a predictive cache.
elements.sent-2411-026: Specifically, we introduce a negative log likelihood (NLL) based instruction pre-population algorithm and establish the relationship between NLL, hit rate and the number of instructions, enabling both hit rate and cache size to be predictable.
elements.sent-2411-027: By leveraging the astounding semantic understanding capability of LLMs, InstCache can predict potential instructions that have not yet been issued, offering functionality beyond traditional caching mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-028: While pre-population, a tree structure is employed to search all possible instructions, where each node is a token and each path is an instruction and its corresponding answer.
elements.sent-2411-029: To maximize the efficiency while inference, after pre-population, InstCache will be flattened into a hash table, mapping hashed instructions to their answers.
elements.sent-2411-030: The hash table minimizes the lookup complexity to nearly $\mathcal{O (1)$.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Experimental results demonstrate that InstCache can achieve a hit rate of up to 51.34\% on LMSys dataset by using approximately 4.5GB of CPU memory, indicating that half of user instructions can be processed using only a CPU and its memory, saving significant energy footprint.
elements.sent-2411-032: When deploying InstCache with existing LLM serving systems, such as vLLM\cite{vllm , we observe up to 2x average speedup on LMSys datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Our contributions are as follows: (1) we propose InstCache which contains predictive user instructions from an LLM, providing the capability exceeding traditional cache mechanism which only reuse requested instructions.
elements.sent-2411-034: In this section, we briefly introduce the concept of a Language Model (LM) and the negative log likelihood (NLL) metric.
elements.sent-2411-035: We explain the basic mechanism of an LM and how it can be used to predict all possible sentences.
elements.sent-2411-036: Large Language Models (LLMs) are designed to autoregressively predict the next token in a sequence based on the preceding tokens.
elements.sent-2411-037: For an LM with a vocabulary size of $v$, the model processes a sequence of tokens $\{t_1, t_2, ..., t_{n-1}\}$ and predicts the probability distribution $p$ over possible tokens in the vocabulary, as described in Eq. \ref{eq:language_model . \begin{equation}p(t_n = V_i|t_1, t_2, ..., t_{n-1}) = LM(t_1, t_2, ..., t_{n-1}) \label{eq:language_model}\end{equation} where $V_i$ represents the $i^{th}$ token in the vocabulary.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}p(t_n = V_i|t_1, t_2, ..., t_{n-1}) = LM(t_1, t_2, ..., t_{n-1}) \label{eq:language_model}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-038: For a sentence $s$ of length $n$, the NLL, which quantifies the impossibility of the sentence, can be computed as shown in Eq. \ref{eq:nll . \begin{equation}NLL(s, LM) = -\ln(\prod^{n}_{i=1}{p(t_i|t_1,t_2,...,t_{i-1})}) \label{eq:nll}\end{equation} Generally, LLMs are trained by padding each passage with special tokens that mark the beginning and end of the passage, such as \textless bos\textgreater{ and \textless eos\textgreater.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Starting with a begin-of-sentence token, an LLM predicts tokens step-by-step, forming possible sequences.
elements.sent-2411-040: The autoregressive scheme enables the prediction of all valid sentences with an NLL below a certain threshold, as required by the proposed work elaborated in Section \ref{sec:instcache .}
elements.subsection-2411-002: Analysis of User Instructions to LLM
elements.sent-2411-041: In this section, we analyze real-world user instructions and identify common patterns, namely, shortness, repetitiveness, and predictability.
elements.sent-2411-042: Due to the scarcity of public datasets of human instructions, we used all available datasets\cite{dataset_survey , LMSys\cite{lmsys and ShareGPT\cite{sharegpt , for analysis.}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: LMSys is a large-scale, real-world conversation dataset collected from the Vicuna demo and the Chatbot Arena website\cite{arena .}
elements.sent-2411-044: The dataset is unfiltered and rich of simpler questions.
elements.sent-2411-045: ShareGPT is derived from conversations shared by GPT users.
elements.sent-2411-046: While capturing more realistic dialogues, ShareGPT has a bias towards harder questions since users often prefer sharing complex and interesting interactions.
elements.sent-2411-047: Although there are various supervised finetuning datasets, such as Moss\cite{moss , they are primarily consist of human instructions and synthetic instructions.}
elements.sent-2411-048: To avoid contamination, we do not use these datasets in this study.
elements.sent-2411-049: Our experiments focus on first-turn instructions, while instructions in more turns follow similar patterns in general.
elements.sent-2411-050: We first analyze the length distribution of instructions in each dataset.
elements.sent-2411-051: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:patterns (a), the majority of user instructions are relatively short, with most containing fewer than 100 tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-052: This is consistent with intuitions, as LLM services are often used in chatbot scenarios\cite{openai , where users interactively input instructions, typically word by word.}
elements.sent-2411-053: When not requiring specific tasks such as text rewriting, users tend to minimize the length of their inputs.
elements.sent-2411-054: While LMSys and ShareGPT datasets may exhibit biases, we believe that short questions still constitute a significant portion of real-world instructions.
elements.sent-2411-055: Optimizing the processing of short instructions can free system resources, thus improving overall system performance.
elements.sent-2411-056: Consequently, in this work, we focus our efforts on optimizing for these short instructions.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{pic/PreliminaryPatterns.pdf} \caption{Analysis of Frequencies on LMSys and ShareGPT datasets shows that a large portion of instructions repeat at least once. The cumulative distribution function of the negative log likelihood of instructions indicates that the majority of instructions are possible to be predicted by LLMs.} \label{fig:preliminary_patterns}
elements.sent-2411-057: Figure \ref{fig:preliminary_patterns (a) illustrates the repetition rates of instructions across datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-058: In LMSys dataset, at least 50\% of instructions are repeated at least once, while in ShareGPT, approximately 30\% are repeated.
elements.sent-2411-059: These findings suggest that users frequently submit similar instructions, which underscores the potential benefits of caching frequent instructions to reduce computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-060: To measure the predictability of user instructions, we compute the negative log likelihood (NLL) of instructions from both datasets using Llama3-8B\cite{llama3 and plot the cumulative distribution functions.}
elements.sent-2411-061: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:preliminary_patterns (b), the NLLs of most instructions saturate fast with relatively small values, indicating that such instructions can be easily predicted by LLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Moreover, as demonstrated in Figure \ref{fig:hit_rate (a), finetuning the LLM on instructions in training set can shift the NLL cumulative distribution further left, improving the predictability of user instructions.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Based on these observations, we propose to predict short instructions using an instruction-aligned LLM.
elements.sent-2411-064: By leverage the semantic capability of LLMs, we can predict potential instructions rather than reuse issued instructions, providing significent enhancement to the traditional caching mechanism.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{pic/Overview.pdf} \caption{Unlike traditional caches that populate with incoming requests while being deployed, InstCache decouples the population phase and deployment phase. During pre-population, InstCache will be populated with all LLM-predicted instructions with negative log likelihood below a selected threshold $\sigma$. The hit rate and cache size can be accurately determined by the threshold $\sigma$. We search these instructions by generating next tokens with an LLM step by step , forming a tree structure, as depicted on the right of figure. The Inst-Aligned LLM is an LLM finetuned with instruction in the training set. After pre-population, each path of the tree will become an instruction stored in hash table with corresponding answer. Lastly, InstCache will be deployed with existing LLM serving systems, accelerating the inference process.} \label{fig:overview}
elements.sent-2411-065: In this section, we outline InstCache, an efficient caching system for LLM inference.
elements.sent-2411-066: As depicted in Figure \ref{fig:overview , InstCache will be pre-populated with LLM-predicted instructions and then deployed with existing LLM serving systems.}
elements.sent-2411-067: In the remaining part of this section, we describe the cache structure in Section \ref{sec:cache_structure and detail the approach to control the hit rate and cache size by a given negative log likelihood (NLL) threshold $\sigma$ in Section \ref{sec:cache_sizing .}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Cache pre-population method is described in Section \ref{sec:cache_prepopulation , and the deployment of InstCache along with existing LLM serving systems is in Section \ref{sec:cache_deployment .}}
elements.sent-2411-069: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:overview , during pre-population, we employ a tree to search potential instruction, where each node represents a token and each path corresponds to an instruction and its answer.}
elements.sent-2411-070: Paths start from begin-of-sentence token \textless bos\textgreater{ and end with end-of-sentence token \textless eos\textgreater.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Answers are stored following the \textless eos\textgreater{ token of each path.}
elements.sent-2411-072: After LLM tree search, each instruction and its corresponding answer will be stored in a hash table, which minimizes the lookup complexity to nearly $\mathcal{O (1)$, facilitating the deployment phase.}
elements.sent-2411-073: As for implementation, we use the dictionary in Python as the hash table during deployment and serialize it to disk with Pickle.
elements.sent-2411-074: Cache size, which can be roughly represented as the number of cached instructions, is a critical parameter.
elements.sent-2411-075: In this sub-section, we develop theoretic analysis on how to determine the cache size.
elements.sent-2411-076: The motivation to pre-populate the cache with LLM-predicted instructions having NLLs below a selected threshold $\sigma$ is to make the cache size and hit rate predictable, by modeling the relationship between NLL, hit rate and the number of instructions.
elements.sent-2411-077: The cache containing all LLM-predicted instructions with NLL $ \leq \sigma$ can be formulated as an instruction set $C=\{s: \text{NLL (s, \text{LM ) \leq \sigma, s \in S\}$, where $s$ represents an instruction, $S$ is the instruction space and $\text{LM}$ is the language model.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Given an instruction designated with random variable $\mathcal{S}$ and its corresponding NLL variable $\mathcal{N =\text{NLL (\mathcal{S , \text{LM )$, the hit rate of the cache can be derived as: \begin{equation}Hit\_Rate = P(S \in C) \notag = P(N \leq \sigma) \notag = F_{N}(\sigma) \label{eq:hit_rate}\end{equation} where $F_{\mathcal{N }$ is the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the NLLs over the instruction space $S$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: The CDF can be measured by evaluating NLLs of instructions on a large validation set using an LLM.
elements.sent-2411-080: Figure \ref{fig:preliminary_patterns (b) has shown the NLL CDF of the ShareGPT dataset with Llama3-8B, indicating that NLLs of most instructions saturate fast with relatively small values.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Therefore, it's possible to build a cache with all instructions having NLLs below a threshold.
elements.sent-2411-082: Furthermore, based on the observation that the number of instructions predicted by an LLM with NLL $\leq \sigma$ grows exponentially with respect to $\sigma$, we propose to model the number of instructions $N$ as an exponential function of the NLL, as shown in Eq. \ref{eq:num_modeling . \begin{equation}N = a(e^{b \sigma} - 1) \label{eq:num_modeling}\end{equation} where $a$ and $b$ are function parameters and can be estimated by preliminary pre-populations under two small $\sigma$ values.}
elements.sent-2411-083: In Figure \ref{fig:hit_rate (a), we show that this exponential model accurately captures the growth of instructions as a function of NLL.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Although the number of instructions grows exponentially, pre-populating a cache with high hit rate does not require too many instructions as the NLL CDF is fast saturated.
elements.sent-2411-085: Since the cache sizing is not accurately while hit rate is, we do not modeling the relationship between hit rate and cache size directly.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{LLM Tree Search} \label{alg:tree_search} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Input:} LLM, Max NLL $\sigma$, Max Depth $d$ \STATE Initialize an empty queue $Q$ \STATE Initialize root $r$ with \textless bos\textgreater token \STATE Enqueue $r$ into $Q$ \WHILE{$Q$ is not empty} \STATE Dequeue a node $u$ from $Q$ \STATE Generate next tokens $T$ for $u$ with an LLM \FOR{each token $t$ of $T$} \IF{Length from $r \to u$ $\le$ $d$ and NLL from $r \to t$ $\leq$ $\sigma$} \STATE Add $t$ as a child of $u$ \ENDIF \STATE Enqueue children of $u$ into $Q$ \ENDFOR \ENDWHILE \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-086: Unlike traditional caches that are populated with incoming requests, InstCache is pre-populated by LLM-predicted instructions with NLL values below a selected threshold.
elements.sent-2411-087: Pre-population involves predicting all possible instructions with NLL $\leq \sigma$ by exploring all paths in a tree that starts with the \textless bos\textgreater{ token and ends with \textless eos\textgreater{ token.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: As illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:overview and Algorithm \ref{alg:tree_search , we use an instruction-aligned LLM to predict the next token at each node of the tree, expanding the tree step by step.}}
elements.sent-2411-089: If a path fails to reach \textless eos\textgreater{ in maximum allowed depth, it will be pruned.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Since NLL is the cumulative negative log probability of tokens along the path, we can optimize the search by stopping the expansion if the cumulative NLL exceeds $\sigma$, reducing computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-091: This allows us to predict all potential instructions within the desired threshold efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-092: To further optimize the process, we limit the number of tokens generated per node during pre-population.
elements.sent-2411-093: For nodes with a depth of 10 or less, we restrict the number of generated tokens to 5,000.
elements.sent-2411-094: For nodes with a depth of up to 100, the limit is reduced to 1,000 tokens.
elements.sent-2411-095: These restrictions significantly reduce overhead, particularly when selecting top-k tokens from a large vocabulary, with a minimal influence on hit rate.
elements.sent-2411-096: Given the exponential growth rate of instructions under increasing NLL, Algorithm \ref{alg:tree_search alone is not scalable for constructing a cache with a high hit rate.}
elements.sent-2411-097: We then proposed a parallel solution for instruction pre-population.
elements.sent-2411-098: We first construct a base tree with a limited depth less than maximal depth and the same maximal NLL value $\sigma$ in master server.
elements.sent-2411-099: Note that we do not prune paths that do not end before limited depth, since they serve as starting nodes for subtrees.
elements.sent-2411-100: For each path of the base tree, we pre-populate a corresponding subtree using Algorithm \ref{alg:tree_search in slave servers, with the path from base tree serving as the root of the corresponding subtree.}
elements.sent-2411-101: As illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:dist_generation , we employ a message queue and remote storage to manage the distributive execution of pre-population.}
elements.sent-2411-102: The subtrees are iteratively merged with the base tree, forming new base tree for subsequent pre-population until the maximum depth is reached.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.99\linewidth]{pic/DistPrepopulation.pdf} \caption{Distributed Cache Pre-population. Master server build the base tree with a limited depth less than maximal depth. Each path of base tree serves as the starting nodes for subtrees generation in slaves. Subtrees are iteratively merged with based tree, forming new base tree for subsequent pre-population, until the maximum depth is reached.} \label{fig:dist_generation}
elements.sent-2411-103: To further decrease the lookup latency of InstCache, we store pre-populated instructions and corresponding answers as a hash table.
elements.sent-2411-104: Upon cache hits, InstCache works as a hash table that maps instructions to their corresponding answers .
elements.sent-2411-105: InstCache can be integrated with existing LLM serving systems such as vLLM\cite{vllm and SGLang\cite{sglang , as depicted in Figure \ref{fig:overview .}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: When an instruction is found in the cache, the corresponding response is immediately returned to the user.
elements.sent-2411-107: If a cache miss occurs, the instruction is forwarded to the LLM serving system for processing.
elements.sent-2411-108: The additional latency introduced by InstCache is negligible as the lookup complexity is near $O(1)$.
elements.sent-2411-109: By leveraging distributive key-value database, such as Redis, InstCache is also well-suited for distributive deployment, which can further increase the hit rate through pre-populating more instructions in distributed storage.

elements.sent-2411-119: GPTCache compares instruction embeddings for matching and thus serves as a Semantic Cache.

elements.sent-2411-223: In this paper, we propose InstCache, an efficient predictive cache system for LLM inference serving.

elements.sent-2411-228: The revolutionary capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) are attracting rapidly growing popularity and leading to soaring user requests to inference serving systems.
elements.sent-2411-229: Caching techniques, which leverage data reuse to reduce computation, offer opportunities to optimize the performance of LLM inference engines.
elements.sent-2411-230: On the one hand, the low-level key-value (KV) cache working at the token level is widely adopted, albeit it incurs significant overhead as request volume grows.
elements.sent-2411-231: On the other hand, instruction-level caching, which stores full instruction-response pairs, is expected to play an increasingly crucial role.
elements.sent-2411-232: However, the high variability in the content and length of instructions make it rare for identical instructions to recur within a short time window, presenting challenges for effective caching instruction-response pairs.
elements.sent-2411-233: To address this challenge, we propose InstCache, a predictive caching mechanism for LLM serving systems.
elements.sent-2411-234: Leveraging the capability of LLMs, we can effectively reorder the representation space of instruction texts and develop a sufficient level of spatial locality.
elements.sent-2411-235: Such spatial locality enables us to predict potential instructions located in a compact region in the space, resulting in an effective caching system at runtime.
elements.sent-2411-236: Experimental results demonstrate that InstCache achieves a 2.3Ă— higher hit rate compared to the upper bound of traditional caching mechanisms on WildChat dataset and reduces the time per output token of vLLM by up to 42.0\% and 50.0\% on LMSys and Moss datasets, respectively.\footnote{Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/InstCache-1244/.}
elements.sent-2411-237: With the rapid growth of demands from a fast increasing number of users and applications, the design of efficient serving system has become a critical challenge.
elements.sent-2411-238: For example, ChatGPT\citep{openai has reached 400 million weekly active users in February 2025 and is expected to exceed one billion by the end of this year.\footnote{https://www.demandsage.com/chatgpt-statistics/ Caching, as an effective method trading storage for computation, has long been a cornerstone of optimizing computing systems and received substantial attention in enhancing the performance of LLM serving systems.}}

elements.sent-2411-240: At a higher level, instructionâ€“response cache stores complete answers to frequently asked queries.
elements.sent-2411-241: As LLM requirements scale, instruction-response cache is expected to play an even more important role to enable computation savings.
elements.sent-2411-242: The unique characteristics of LLM instructions, however, hinders the efficiency of adopting such cache structures in real-world LLM inference serving systems.

elements.sent-2411-262: To address this challenge, we propose to leverage the intrinsic semantic capability of LLMs to reorder the text space and develop a sufficient level of spatial locality.

elements.sent-2411-266: Conceptually, the LLM acts analogously to serving as a memory address remapping mechanism, facilitating continuous memory access and effective prefetching by transforming sparse accesses into a denser access pattern.

elements.sent-2411-295: Given a language model with a vocabulary size of $V$ and a maximum token length $L$, the entire text space can be represented as paths in a V-ary tree of depth $L$.
elements.sent-2411-296: In the V-ary tree defined by a language model, each node corresponds to a token, and each edge represents the probability of generating the next token given the preceding context.
elements.sent-2411-297: The child nodes of each node are ordered according to their respective generation probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-298: As illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:text_space , each path from the root to a leaf in the tree corresponds to a unique text.}
elements.sent-2411-299: Notably, each path should begin with <bos> token and end with <eos> token.
elements.sent-2411-300: Text ending with <eos> will not be expanded further.
elements.sent-2411-301: For simplicity, all paths shown in Figure \ref{fig:text_space have the same length.}
elements.sent-2411-302: Given the text space, we use the Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL), defined as $\text{NLL =-ln(\prod_{i=1 ^{n {p(t_i|t_1,t_2,...,t_{i-1 ) )$ to represent the position of a text within the space, analogous to a memory address.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-303: Given a specific language model, this induces a distribution of texts over a one-dimensional space.
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{figures/text_space.pdf} \caption{Text space can be represented as paths in a V-ary tree of depth $L$, constructed by a language model with a vocabulary size of $V$ and a maximum token length of $L$. In this V-ary tree, each node corresponds to a token, and each edge represents the probability of generating that token given the preceding context. The child nodes of any given node are ordered by their probabilities. Each path from the root to a leaf defines a unique text, collectively forming a sequential text space. Given the NLL of each path, these text sequences can be projected onto a one-dimensional space. For simplicity, the figure demonstrates a binary tree and assumes that each nodeâ€™s children follow an identical probability distribution. Under this assumption, text2 and text3 share the same NLL.} \label{fig:text_space}
elements.sent-2411-304: When a language model is trained on a specific class of texts, such as user instructions, texts with similar structures and meanings tend to have lower NLL values and are thus concentrated near the "head" of the space.
elements.sent-2411-305: Such a process induces a form of spatial locality, allowing the retrieval of the set of potential instructions by selecting a contiguous region near the beginning of the space.
elements.sent-2411-306: Formally, given a threshold $\sigma$, we define the InstCache as $C=\{\langle s, r \rangle: \text{NLL (s) \leq \sigma, s \in S\}$, where $s$ and $r$ denote an instruction and its corresponding response, and $S$ represents the text space.}
elements.sent-2411-307: The cache therefore includes all instructions with their NLL falling below the threshold, i.e., the most likely ones.
elements.sent-2411-308: This formulation allows us to analytically predict the hit rate with the threshold $\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-309: Let the $\mathcal{S}$ denote a random variable of instruction and $\mathcal{N =\text{NLL (\mathcal{S )$ its associated $\text{NLL}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-310: The expected hit rate of InstCache is then given by: \begin{equation}Hit\_Rate = P(S \in C) \notag = P(N \leq \sigma) \notag = F_{N}(\sigma) \label{eq:hit_rate}\end{equation} where $F_{\mathcal{N }$ is the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of $\mathcal{N}$.}
elements.sent-2411-311: By measuring this CDF on a validation set drawn from recently observed instructions, we can estimate the expected hit rate for a given cache threshold $\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-312: In preliminary experiments, we observe that the next-token probability distribution at each node tends to follow a power-law pattern.
elements.sent-2411-313: We further prove that the number of reachable leaf nodes in the text space grows as an exponential function depending on $\sigma$ when assuming a power-law distribution of the next-token.
elements.sent-2411-314: The formal theorem is stated as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-315: Let $L$ be the length of generated texts, and the generation starts from a \textless bos\textgreater{ token.}
elements.sent-2411-316: Then the total number $N$ of distinct texts of length $L$ that can be generated is given by: $N \approx {e^{\frac{\sigma {\alpha \cdot \left(\frac{\sigma {\alpha}\right)^{L-1 /{(L-1)!}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-317: Details of the power-law pattern observation and proof are provided in Appendix \ref{sec:theorem_prove .}

elements.sent-2411-321: Before pre-populating InstCache, we first train a LLM using observed instructions, padding them with <bos> and <eos> tokens.

elements.sent-2411-323: Afterward, we traverse the V-ary tree step by step to pre-populate InstCache with instructions whose $\text{NLL}$ values are below the threshold $\sigma$ and that end with the <eos> token.
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Instruction Pre-population} \label{alg:tree_search} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Input:} a fine-tuned LLM, Max NLL $\sigma$, Max Length $L$ \STATE { Output:} Pre-populated instructions $C$ \STATE Initialize an empty dequeue $Q$ and empty list $C$ \STATE Initialize root $r$ with <bos> token \STATE Enqueue $r$ into $Q$ \WHILE{$Q$ is not empty} \STATE Dequeue a node $u$ from the head of $Q$ \STATE Generate next tokens $T$ for $u$ with LLM \STATE // Suppose next tokens are sorted in ascending order of probability \FOR{each token $t$ of $T$} \IF{Length from $r \to u$ $<$ $L$ and NLL from $r \to t$ $\leq$ $\sigma$} \IF{$t$ is <eos>} \STATE Decode token sequence from $r$ to $t$ and append to $C$ \ELSE \STATE Add $t$ as a child of $u$ \STATE Enqueue children node $t$ into the head of $Q$ \ENDIF \ENDIF \ENDFOR \ENDWHILE \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-324: As shown in Algorithm \ref{alg:tree_search , the tree is traversed in a depth-first manner.}
elements.sent-2411-325: At each step, the model generates tokens for the current node.
elements.sent-2411-326: Due to the cumulative nature of NLL, we can prune child nodes with their NLLs exceeding the threshold, thus avoiding unnecessary computations.
elements.sent-2411-327: To minimize redundant recomputation, the key-value states associated with each node are preserved during traversal until all its descendants have been explored.

elements.sent-2411-400: Figure \ref{fig:prepopulation_time shows the pre-population time with varying numbers of instruction on WildChat dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-401: As observed, the computational cost scales linearly with the number of instructions.
elements.sent-2411-402: Notably, the distributed approach has a fixed overhead incurred when preparing a small V-ary tree, which must be constructed on a single GPU.
elements.sent-2411-403: Additionally, task distribution across GPUs also introduces a modest load-balancing overhead.
elements.sent-2411-404: Therefore, as the task size grows, these overheads are effectively amortized, and the efficiency of distributed pre-population gradually approaches the theoretical speedup corresponding to the number of GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-405: Figure \ref{fig:max_kvcache_mem demonstrates the relationship between the GPU memory footprint of the KVCache in our pre-population system and the number of instructions.}
elements.sent-2411-406: As the number of instructions increases, the growth rate of the KVCache memory usage on the GPU gradually slow down, validating the effectiveness of our deep first searching strategy, which minimizes the overhead associated with preserving KVCache for existing nodes in the V-ary tree.
elements.sent-2411-407: Moreover, the KVCache memory overhead can be further reduced by decreasing the batch size.
elements.sent-2411-408: As user instruction distributions may shift over time, the hit rate of a static InstCache will gradually decline.
elements.sent-2411-409: However, benefited from the semantic capability of LLM for cache construction, InstCache can still capture instruction similarity despite temporal drift.
elements.sent-2411-410: Since the WildChat dataset includes timestamps spanning an entire year, we first train the LLM on the data from the first six months.
elements.sent-2411-411: After that, we evaluate the hit rate with different NLLs on the test datasets from the first six months and the subsequent months.
elements.sent-2411-412: As illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:dynamic , the experimental results indicate that as the temporal gap between the training and testing datasets increases, InstCache performance gradually degrades.}
elements.sent-2411-413: Nevertheless, the decline remains steady over time, proving that most popular instructions tend to remain stable and do not change frequently.
elements.sent-2411-414: Since InstCache is a static cache, gradual shifts in the instruction distribution can cause a decline in hit rates, as demonstrated in Section \ref{sec:distribution_shift .}
elements.sent-2411-415: Therefore, periodic cache updates are necessary to maintain an up-to-date representation of the instruction distribution.
elements.sent-2411-416: Thanks to the highly optimized tree-searching algorithm, the pre-population overhead remains relatively low.
elements.sent-2411-417: Moreover, existing responses to observed instructions and previously pre-populated instructions can be reused in subsequent pre-population cycles if they share common instructions, further reducing the overall cost.
elements.sent-2411-418: In addition to common conversational applications, InstCache also supports tasks that involve post-processing of user prompts, such as Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)\citep{RAG .}
elements.sent-2411-419: For RAG tasks, InstCache only predicts user-written instructions and then appends additional documents retrieved from the database to generate responses.
elements.sent-2411-420: For use cases that requires sampling different responses to the same instruction, InstCache can be used at the initial stage, while subsequent instructions from the same user within a short time period can bypass the cache.
elements.sent-2411-421: Of course, instruction-response caching mechanisms are not proper for instructions with high timeliness.
elements.sent-2411-422: Finally, extra preprocessing can be necessary before fitting the instruction distribution with observed instructions.
elements.sent-2411-423: In this paper, we remove any instructions containing personal information, such as names, locations, and other sensitive details.
elements.sent-2411-424: In real-world deployment scenarios, harmful or personal instructions can be filtered out after pre-population so as to minimize the risk of data leakage.
elements.sent-2411-425: In this paper, we address the challenge of dissipated locality in instruction-response caching systems by leveraging the semantic capability of LLM to reorder the text space and develop spatial locality.
elements.sent-2411-426: Utilizing the spatial locality, we can predict potential instructions and construct the InstCache.
elements.sent-2411-427: Furthermore, we demonstrate that the hit rate and cache size of InstCache can be predicted.
elements.sent-2411-428: To efficiently pre-populate InstCache, we develop a depth-first V-ary tree search algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-429: Experimental results validate the effectiveness of InstCache.
elements.sent-2411-430: In the future, we will develop a distributed implementation of the caching system on larger computer clusters to further enhance the performance.. \bibliographystyle{plain .}
